difficulty_factors: Four-component combination stain: cocoa butter (oil), milk protein, cocoa tannin, cocoa starch
Requires solid removal before any chemistry — liquid on solid chocolate spreads it
Heat melts cocoa butter deeper AND sets protein simultaneously
White cotton allows full chemistry arsenal — forgiving fabric

diy.title: Chocolate on White Cotton
ingredients: Enzyme detergent (Tide or Zout — for milk protein and starch)
mixSteps: Let melted chocolate cool and solidify before scraping
Mix ¼ tablespoon enzyme detergent with 2 oz cool water
Have dish soap, vinegar, peroxide, and ammonia ready in sequence

steps.instruction: Let melted chocolate solidify. Scrape off all solid chocolate with a dull knife or spoon edge. Work in one direction — don't smear back and forth. Remove maximum solid before any liquid.
steps.why: Chocolate that's still solid can be physically removed without chemistry. Applying liquid to solid chocolate melts cocoa butter and drives it into the fiber — making a small stain much larger. Scrape gently — you're removing the bulk so chemistry can focus on what's bonded to the fiber.

steps.instruction: Turn the fabric over and flush COLD water from the back, pushing the stain out rather than deeper in.
steps.why: Cold water flushes dissolved cocoa solids out through the fabric. Hot water would melt cocoa butter deeper in AND cook the milk protein — a double disaster. Dan Eisen: first wash must not exceed 100°F for protein stains.

steps.instruction: Apply a drop of Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work in gently with your finger or a soft brush. Let sit 5 minutes. Rinse with cool water.
steps.why: Dawn's surfactant emulsifies cocoa butter (the oil/fat component). This is the POG step in professional spotting — addressing the dry-side component before wet-side chemistry. On white cotton, dish soap is safe and effective for triglyceride fats.

steps.instruction: Apply enzyme detergent solution (Tide or Zout mixed with cool water). Work in gently. Let sit 15–30 minutes.
steps.why: Protease digests the casein protein from milk chocolate. Amylase digests the cocoa starch. Zout is ideal here — its triple enzyme system (protease + amylase + lipase) handles all three biological components. The lipase provides additional fat digestion as backup for the dish soap step. Safe on cotton — cotton is cellulose, not protein.

steps.instruction: Rinse with cool water. Apply white vinegar mixed with a little detergent. Work into the stain. Let sit 3 minutes. Rinse.
steps.why: Vinegar (acetic acid) breaks hydrogen bonds between cocoa tannin polyphenols and cotton cellulose. This is the tannin step — must come after oil and protein are addressed. If protein residue remained when acid was applied, it could cross-link the protein.

steps.instruction: If brown discoloration persists: apply hydrogen peroxide 3%, add 2–3 drops ammonia. Wait 3 minutes. Rinse with water. Apply vinegar. Rinse again.
steps.why: Peroxide + ammonia oxidizes the cocoa tannin chromophore — the brown color. Ammonia catalyzes peroxide into the more reactive perhydroxyl anion. Vinegar neutralizes the alkali and prevents yellowing. Dan Eisen uses this exact sequence for combination stain residual color.

steps.instruction: Wash in warm water with enzyme detergent. Air dry and inspect. Do NOT use dryer until stain is confirmed completely gone.
steps.why: Dryer heat (140°F+) permanently sets any remaining tannin. Air dry first, verify, then launder normally.

diy.ifStillThere: Soak in OxiClean solution (1 scoop per gallon warm water) for 1–6 hours. For white cotton specifically: dilute chlorine bleach (1:10) is safe as a nuclear option — soak 15 minutes, rinse, vinegar rinse. If stain survives bleach, it's polymerized past home treatment — professional spotting required.
proTips: SOLIDS FIRST. Always. Liquid on solid chocolate = bigger stain.
Milk chocolate has more protein and fat. Dark chocolate has more tannin and less protein. White chocolate is almost all fat (no tannin).
Zout Triple Enzyme is the best single product for chocolate — one spray addresses protein (protease), starch (amylase), and fat (lipase) simultaneously.
Dan Eisen's combination stain article (#19): chocolate is a textbook combination of tannin + protein. The key is treating each component in order without setting the others.
diy.safety: Test ammonia and peroxide on hidden seam even on white cotton — some have optical brighteners. NEVER mix ammonia with chlorine bleach. Well-ventilated area for ammonia use.
diy.whyThisWorks: Chocolate is the textbook combination stain: cocoa butter (fat requiring surfactant), milk solids (protein requiring enzyme), cocoa starch (requiring amylase), and cocoa polyphenols (tannin requiring acid + oxidizer). Each component is treated in sequence — oil → protein → starch → tannin → residual color. On white cotton, the full chemical arsenal is available, giving the highest success rate.

home.title: Chocolate on White Cotton — Confident Removal
home.confidenceNote: White cotton is the most forgiving fabric. Fresh chocolate responds well. Aged chocolate may need enzyme soak + OxiClean treatment.

primaryProduct.name: Zout Triple Enzyme Stain Remover
primaryProduct.why: Triple enzyme addresses all three biological components: protease (milk protein), lipase (cocoa butter), amylase (cocoa starch)
primaryProduct.amazonSearchQuery: Zout triple enzyme stain remover spray laundry

alternativeProducts.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
alternativeProducts.why: Oxygen bleach for the cocoa tannin color after enzyme treatment

alternativeProducts.name: Dawn Ultra Dishwashing Liquid (Original)
alternativeProducts.why: Effective surfactant specifically for the cocoa butter/fat component

home.prepStep: Let chocolate solidify, then scrape off all solids. COLD water only — heat melts cocoa butter deeper and sets protein.

method.instruction: Scrape off all solid chocolate with a dull knife. Flush from back with cold water.
method.why: Physical removal first. Cold water from back pushes stain out, not deeper.

method.instruction: Apply a drop of Dawn directly to stain. Work gently. Rinse with cool water after 5 minutes.
method.why: Dawn's surfactant emulsifies cocoa butter — the fat component that carries other stain components deeper.

method.instruction: Spray Zout Triple Enzyme onto the stain. Let sit 15–30 minutes.
method.why: Three enzymes work simultaneously: protease on milk protein, lipase on residual fat, amylase on cocoa starch.

method.instruction: Rinse. If brown color remains, make OxiClean paste and apply. Let sit 15–30 minutes. Rinse.
method.why: Oxygen bleach oxidizes the cocoa tannin chromophore — the brown color left after protein and fat are removed.

method.instruction: Wash warm with enzyme detergent. Air dry. Inspect before using dryer.
method.why: Heat from dryer permanently sets residual tannin.

materialWarnings.warning: Full chemistry is safe — enzymes, OxiClean, peroxide, dilute chlorine bleach as last resort.
COLD water until protein is addressed — heat sets milk protein permanently.
DANGER: Never mix ammonia with chlorine bleach. Produces toxic chloramine gas.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: Enzyme soak (Zout in warm water, 1 hour) followed by OxiClean soak (1–6 hours). Last resort on white cotton: dilute chlorine bleach (1:10), 15 minutes, thorough rinse.

proTips: Zout is the single best consumer product for chocolate — three enzymes for three biological components.
Dark chocolate: skip the protein step, focus on tannin. White chocolate: skip the tannin step, focus on fat.
home.whyThisWorks: Sequential treatment of each chocolate component: surfactant for fat, enzyme for protein and starch, oxidizer for tannin color. White cotton tolerates the full chemical arsenal.
home.timeEstimate: 25–45 min (may need enzyme soak for dried chocolate)

pro.title: Chocolate on White Cotton — Professional Combination Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (oil + protein + tannin + starch). The sequence rules define chocolate as: scrape solid → cold water flush → protease for milk protein → amylase for cocoa starch → POG/NSD for cocoa butter → tannin formula for cocoa polyphenols → H₂O₂ for residual brown. Four distinct chemical phases. Dan Eisen combination stain article (#19): chocolate is the exemplar combination stain. White cotton: full chemistry safe. pH 6.0–11.0. Heat to 95°C. All enzymes, all solvents, chlorine bleach (diluted, whites).

spottingProtocol.technique: Spatula/dull knife — remove all surface solids before chemistry
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape off all solid chocolate. If melted, let cool first. Per sequence rule SOLIDS_REMOVED_FIRST: 'Liquids spread stain; mechanical removal first minimizes the stain footprint.' On the spotting board, use spatula working in one direction.

spottingProtocol.technique: Water gun at safe distance — no steam until protein is cleared
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold water gun, 6+ inches, from back of fabric. No steam — cocoa butter melts at body temperature and steam drives it deep. Milk protein sets at 40°C+. Cold flush removes dissolved cocoa solids.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent for cocoa butter — dry-side before wet-side
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG to dissolve cocoa butter (triglyceride fat). Per sequence rule DRY_SIDE_BEFORE_WET_SIDE and RR Street's combination approach: 'Begin with dry-side treatment, finish with wet-side.' The cocoa butter creates a hydrophobic barrier — if you skip this step, water-based enzyme chemistry can't penetrate to the protein. Tamp gently. Flush with VDS or steam (now acceptable — POG has already addressed the fat layer).

spottingProtocol.technique: Enzyme digestion of casein protein from milk chocolate
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply protease enzyme at 30–40°C. Safe on cotton (cellulose fiber — protease only attacks protein). Per Dan Eisen's protein article (#88): 'In order for enzymes to work they must be used in warm water (120°F). They must be allowed to remain on the stain for at least ½ hour.' Enzyme converts insoluble protein to soluble peptides that flush with water. For milk chocolate, this step is essential. For dark chocolate (minimal milk), this step can be shortened.

spottingProtocol.agent: Amylase enzyme (can combine with protease soak)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Amylase for the starch component of cocoa. Can be combined with the protease soak — amylase is safe on all fibers. Zout or Tide contain both. Starch is a minor component but contributes to the stain matrix holding everything to the fiber.

spottingProtocol.technique: Acidic tannin spotter for cocoa polyphenol pigment
spottingProtocol.instruction: After flushing enzyme residue: apply tannin formula. The brown color remaining after oil and protein treatment is cocoa tannin — polyphenols from Theobroma cacao. Acidic pH breaks hydrogen bonds between tannin and cotton cellulose. Tamp with brush. Flush with water. Follow with neutral lubricant or protein formula to neutralize acid on cellulose (NEUTRALIZE_ACID_AFTER_TANNIN rule).

spottingProtocol.technique: Oxidizing bleach for residual brown chromophore
spottingProtocol.instruction: ONLY after fully flushing tannin formula. Apply H₂O₂ 6% + 1 drop 28% ammonia per 8 oz. Safe on white cotton. Oxidizes remaining cocoa tannin chromophore. Flush and neutralize with acetic acid.
Apply acetic acid (vinegar). Neutralizes ammonia alkalinity. Flush thoroughly. Per Dan Eisen: always follow ammonia with vinegar to neutralize and prevent yellowing.

pro.escalation: If stain persists: (1) Sodium percarbonate soak, warm water, 1–6 hours. (2) Chlorine bleach 1:10 on white cotton, 15 min max. (3) Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) for polymerized cocoa tannin. Professional assessment recommended at this point.
pro.customerExplanation: Chocolate is four stains in one: cocoa butter (fat), milk protein, cocoa starch, and cocoa pigment (tannin). We treated each in order — solvent for the fat, enzymes for the protein and starch, acid for the tannin, and oxidation for the remaining brown color. White cotton tolerates all of these treatments safely.
commonMistakes: Applying liquid before scraping solids — spreads cocoa butter and enlarges the stain
Using hot water before protein treatment — sets casein and melts cocoa butter deeper
Skipping the POG/fat step — protein enzyme can't penetrate through the cocoa butter barrier
Applying peroxide before flushing tannin formula — oxidizes tannin in place permanently
Using alcohol-based products — sets the protein component (Dan Eisen: 'alcohol will SET blood and protein stains')
pro.neutralization: Acetic acid after alkaline steps. Neutral lubricant after tannin formula (protect cellulose from acid). Full water flush between every chemical change.
pro.fabricRisks: White cotton: pH 6.0–11.0. Heat to 95°C. All enzymes safe. All solvents safe. Chlorine bleach safe (diluted, whites only). Maximum flexibility for this stain.
